title: Chia Pudding with Protein Powder (27g Protein, Make-Ahead Breakfast) | Eati
introduction: Chia pudding with protein powder is a cold, creamy, 290-calorie breakfast with 27g of protein and 12g of fiber — and it takes 2 minutes to prepare the night before. The chia seeds absorb almond milk overnight into a tapioca-like texture that feels like dessert but fuels your morning like a clean meal.

sections.heading: Ingredients (1 serving)
sections.content:
- 3 tablespoons (30g) chia seeds
- 1 scoop (30g) whey or casein protein powder (vanilla)
- 1 cup (240ml) unsweetened almond milk
- 1/2 tsp vanilla extract
- Pinch of salt
- 1/2 cup (75g) mixed berries (to top)
- Optional: 1 tsp maple syrup or honey, 1 tbsp nut butter, cinnamon

sections.heading: Instructions
sections.content:
1. In a mason jar or container, combine chia seeds, protein powder, and salt.
2. Whisk in almond milk and vanilla extract until no clumps remain.
3. Let sit 10 minutes, then whisk again (prevents clumping at the bottom).
4. Cover and refrigerate at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
5. Stir in the morning. Top with berries and optional maple syrup.
6. Log your serving in Eati.

sections.heading: Calories, Fat, and Protein (Estimated)
sections.content:
Calories: 290 kcal per serving (base recipe)
Fat: 10g
Protein: 27g
Carbs: 22g (12g fiber, 5g natural sugars)

With 1 tbsp peanut butter + 1 tsp honey: 395 cal, 30g protein. With extra 1 tbsp chia: +60 cal, +3g protein, +5g fiber.

sections.heading: Why Chia Pudding Is a Fat-Loss Secret
sections.content:
Chia pudding is one of the few breakfasts that naturally fills you up while keeping calories in check. Here's what the science shows:

Macro breakdown:
• 290 calories
• 27g protein (complete when combined with whey)
• 10g fat (5g omega-3 ALA from chia)
• 22g carbs with 12g fiber — that's 40%+ DV fiber
• ~9g protein per 100 calories

The chia seed advantage:
• 1 tbsp of chia absorbs up to 10× its weight in liquid.
• 3 tbsp creates enough 'gel' to form a pudding texture.
• Slow-releases liquid and carbs over 3–4 hours.
• Extremely high fiber content (8g per 2 tbsp).

What 12g of fiber does for you:
• Slows gastric emptying (you feel full longer).
• Feeds beneficial gut bacteria.
• Lowers post-meal glucose spikes.
• Supports regular digestion (especially important on a deficit).

Protein synergy:
Adding a scoop of protein powder transforms chia from a nice-to-have topping into a complete meal. Without it: 180 cal, 6g protein (not enough). With it: 290 cal, 27g protein (ideal).

Omega-3 bonus:
• 3 tbsp chia = 5g ALA (plant-based omega-3).
• Your body converts ALA to EPA/DHA at low rates (5–15%), but daily chia still contributes.
• Anti-inflammatory, cardiovascular-supportive.

Casein vs whey in chia pudding:
• Casein: thickens the pudding more, longer satiety (4–6 hrs).
• Whey: mixes smoother, faster protein delivery (post-workout friendly).
• Both work well; choose by preference.

For more make-ahead options, see [overnight oats with protein](/blog/overnight-oats-protein-recipe) and [20 high-protein low-calorie breakfast ideas](/blog/20-high-protein-low-calorie-breakfast-ideas).

sections.heading: Chia Pudding Ratios: Get the Texture Right
sections.content:
The #1 problem with chia pudding is texture — either too runny, too thick, or gritty. Here are the battle-tested ratios:

Perfect ratio cheat sheet:
• Thin pudding (yogurt-like): 2 tbsp chia : 1 cup liquid
• Medium pudding (tapioca-like — default): 3 tbsp chia : 1 cup liquid
• Thick pudding (pudding-like): 4 tbsp chia : 1 cup liquid
• Chia parfait (spoonable): 5 tbsp chia : 1 cup liquid

For 1 serving breakfast: 3 tbsp chia + 1 cup almond milk = ideal texture.

The 2-stir method (critical):
• Whisk ingredients well initially.
• Wait 10 minutes. Whisk AGAIN.
• This prevents chia from sinking and clumping at the bottom.
• Missing this step = pudding with a rock-hard bottom.

Liquid options (each delivers different results):
• Unsweetened almond milk: 30 cal/cup. Lightest, lowest calorie.
• Skim dairy milk: 80 cal/cup, +8g protein. Creamier, more protein.
• Oat milk: 120 cal/cup. Sweetest, adds carbs.
• Coconut milk (light): 40 cal/cup. Tropical flavor, slightly creamy.
• Greek yogurt + milk (1:1): Ultra-creamy, 20g+ extra protein.

Troubleshooting:
• Too thick? Stir in extra 2 tbsp of milk.
• Too thin? Add 1 tsp chia, wait 1 hour.
• Clumpy? Whisk harder initially; breaking up clumps is almost impossible after.
• Gritty or crunchy? Wait longer — minimum 4 hrs, ideally 8+ hrs.

Pro tip:
For ultra-smooth texture, blend all ingredients in a blender for 30 seconds BEFORE refrigerating. The chia seeds stay intact but the powder/liquid gets perfectly integrated.

sections.heading: 10 Chia Pudding Flavor Variations
sections.content:
Chia is a blank canvas. Here are 10 tested variations — all stay around 27g protein:

1. Classic vanilla berry (this recipe, 290 cal, 27g protein):
• Base + vanilla + mixed berries.

2. Chocolate PB (400 cal, 30g protein):
• Chocolate whey + 1 tbsp PB + 1 tbsp cocoa powder + banana slice. Dessert-level.

3. Coconut-mango (330 cal, 27g protein):
• Vanilla whey + 1/2 cup diced mango + 1 tbsp toasted coconut + lime zest.

4. Matcha latte (300 cal, 27g protein):
• Vanilla whey + 1 tsp matcha powder + honey to taste.

5. Pumpkin pie (305 cal, 27g protein):
• Vanilla whey + 2 tbsp pumpkin puree + pumpkin pie spice + 1 tsp maple syrup.

6. Apple cinnamon (310 cal, 27g protein):
• Vanilla whey + 1/4 diced apple + extra cinnamon + raisins.

7. Strawberry shortcake (305 cal, 28g protein):
• Strawberry whey + 1/2 cup strawberries + 1 tbsp Greek yogurt swirl.

8. Tropical piña colada (320 cal, 27g protein):
• Vanilla whey + coconut milk + 1/4 cup pineapple + toasted coconut.

9. Cherry vanilla (300 cal, 27g protein):
• Vanilla whey + 1/2 cup frozen cherries + almond extract.

10. Lemon cheesecake (315 cal, 30g protein):
• Vanilla whey + 2 tbsp cottage cheese + lemon zest + 2 graham cracker crumbs.

Layered parfait style:
• Bottom: chia pudding
• Middle: Greek yogurt or extra berries
• Top: granola (1 tbsp) + drizzle of honey
• Adds 80–120 cal but ups the visual appeal and texture contrast.

Skipping protein powder entirely?
• Swap in 1/2 cup Greek yogurt + 1/4 cup cottage cheese.
• Macros: 250 cal, 20g protein (close to the original but fully food-based).

sections.heading: Meal Prep and Storage Strategy
sections.content:
Chia pudding is possibly the easiest breakfast to batch prep — it actually improves after 24 hours:

Sunday prep (5 jars in 10 min):
1. Line up 5 16oz mason jars.
2. Add to each: 3 tbsp chia + 1 scoop whey + pinch salt.
3. Fill each with 1 cup almond milk + 1/2 tsp vanilla.
4. Seal jars and shake vigorously 30 seconds.
5. Wait 10 min, shake again.
6. Refrigerate.

Day-of topping:
• Add fresh berries or fruit in the morning (fruit doesn't meal-prep as well as chia).
• Store toppings separately in small containers.

Storage times:
• Pre-made chia pudding (sealed): 5 days in fridge.
• With fresh fruit mixed in: 2 days max.
• Never freeze — the chia gel breaks and gets watery.

Smart shortcuts:
• Buy chia seeds in 3 lb bags ($0.30/tbsp vs $1+ for small bottles).
• Use individual-serve almond milk cartons for travel/office storage.
• Pre-portion whey scoops into jars or small containers.

Budget per serving:
• Chia: $0.45
• Whey: $0.80
• Almond milk: $0.40
• Berries: $0.50
• Total: $2.15 per breakfast

Compared to store-bought chia pudding cups ($4–6 each) — you save $2–4 per serving.

Travel options:
• Office breakfast: bring jar to work and keep in fridge, eat within 3 days.
• Flight/travel: pre-make in sealable container; eat within 4 days.
• Camping: chia seeds + shelf-stable almond milk + whey powder. Mix in portable container with water if no milk available.

Body-reaction tip:
Some people get bloated from sudden high-fiber intake (12g fiber per serving is significant). If you're new to chia, start with 2 tbsp and work up to 3 over a week. Drink extra water — chia absorbs a lot.

For more smart meal prep, see [how to track calories correctly](/blog/how-to-track-calories-correctly) and [overnight oats with protein](/blog/overnight-oats-protein-recipe).

midArticleCta: Make breakfast tonight, wake up to 27g of protein. Log this chia pudding in Eati and prove that cold breakfasts can be just as effective.
conclusion: A 2-minute prep, 4-hour set — chia pudding with protein powder becomes one of the easiest high-protein breakfasts in your rotation. 290 calories with 27g protein and 12g fiber beats most grab-and-go options at any price point.
metaTitle: Chia Pudding with Protein Powder: 27g Protein, 290 Calories (Make-Ahead)
metaDescription: Easy chia pudding with protein powder — 27g protein and 12g fiber in 290 calories. Includes perfect ratio guide, 10 flavor variations, and weekly meal prep strategy.

faqs.question: How many calories in chia pudding with protein powder?
faqs.answer: About 290 calories per serving with 3 tbsp chia seeds, 1 scoop whey protein, 1 cup unsweetened almond milk, and 1/2 cup berries. Delivers 27g protein, 12g fiber, and 10g healthy fats.

faqs.question: What's the best ratio of chia seeds to liquid?
faqs.answer: 3 tbsp chia to 1 cup of liquid gives a perfect tapioca-like pudding texture. For thicker pudding use 4 tbsp, for thinner yogurt-like use 2 tbsp. The key is whisking twice — once initially and again 10 minutes later to prevent clumping.

faqs.question: Can I use casein protein in chia pudding?
faqs.answer: Yes — and casein actually works better than whey for chia pudding. Casein thickens the pudding more and provides slower-digesting protein (4–6 hour satiety). Whey gives a smoother texture and faster absorption (good post-workout).

faqs.question: How long does chia pudding last in the fridge?
faqs.answer: Pre-made chia pudding (without fresh fruit mixed in) keeps 5 days refrigerated in a sealed jar. If topped with fresh berries or banana, eat within 2 days. Never freeze — the chia gel structure breaks when thawed.

faqs.question: Is chia pudding good for weight loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. 27g protein plus 12g fiber create strong satiety, and the 290 cal serving size leaves plenty of room for other meals. The soluble fiber slows digestion and reduces afternoon hunger. Just watch the toppings — adding 1/4 cup granola or 2 tbsp nut butter can push calories past 500.
